---
title: "conseguiR Worked Example Report"
author: "The conseguiR authors"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
output:
  BiocStyle::html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    number_sections: true
    df_print: paged
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{conseguiR Worked Example Report}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center",
  out.width = "92%"
)
```

```{r libraries}
library(conseguiR)
library(data.table)
library(knitr)
```

<style>
body {
  line-height: 1.75;
  font-size: 16px;
}
h1, h2, h3 {
  font-weight: 700;
}
.lead {
  font-size: 1.1em;
  color: #4b5563;
}
.callout {
  padding: 1rem 1.2rem;
  border-left: 4px solid #2c7fb8;
  background: #f7fbff;
  margin: 1.2rem 0;
}
</style>

# Executive summary

<div class="lead">
This report presents a worked <code>conseguiR</code> analysis using a Sjogren
syndrome GWAS, a lymphoma-focused PCAWG somatic mutation dataset, and a set of
selected epigenomic bigWig tracks. The goal is not only to show the code, but
also to explain what each stage is contributing and what the resulting figures
look like along the way.
</div>

At a high level, the workflow proceeds in six stages:

1. validate the raw inputs
2. compute germline, somatic, and epigenomic scores
3. impose those scores on the gene-regulatory graph
4. run diffusion to obtain post-diffusion gene scores
5. extract a smaller selected subgraph
6. inspect the results through score plots, network plots, and a locus plot

Because some compute stages are intentionally heavy, the code below is shown as
runnable analysis code but is not evaluated during document render. The figures
shown later in the report come from generated outputs already present in the
project.

# Example inputs

The example analysis uses the following files.

```{r inputs}
gwas_path <- "data/raw/Testing/GCST90436637.h.tsv"
somatic_maf_path <- "data/raw/Testing/2026-01-09_no_CLL_lymph_only_pcawg_maf_tcga_order_hg38.maf"
reg_ref_path <- "data/processed/GRCh38-cCREs.loc"
refdb_path <- "data/raw/Testing/RefCDS_human_GRCh38.p12.rda"
reference_bfile <- "data/raw/g1000_eur/g1000_eur"
track_dir <- "data/raw/Testing"
sample_size <- 399917L

all_bw_files <- list.files(track_dir, pattern = "\\.(bw|bigWig)$", full.names = TRUE)
all_bw_files <- all_bw_files[!grepl("^\\._", basename(all_bw_files))]
bw_files <- all_bw_files[!grepl("(_BL_|_FL_|broken_signal_track)", basename(all_bw_files))]

data.table(
  object = c("GWAS", "Somatic MAF", "Regulatory reference", "dndscv reference", "PLINK reference", "Selected bigWig tracks"),
  value = c(
    gwas_path,
    somatic_maf_path,
    reg_ref_path,
    refdb_path,
    reference_bfile,
    paste(length(bw_files), "tracks")
  )
)
```

<div class="callout">
For this report we keep the epigenomic track filtering explicit in user code.
That makes the vignette easier to read and keeps the package API focused on
general behavior rather than on one lab-specific filtering rule.
</div>

# Step 1: validate the inputs

Before running any heavy computation, we usually make sure the basic file and
column structure is sound.

```{r validate-code, eval=FALSE}
validation <- validate_inputs(
  gwas_sumstats = gwas_path,
  somatic_maf = somatic_maf_path,
  reg_ref_path = reg_ref_path,
  epigenomic_tracks = bw_files,
  verbose = TRUE
)
```

The validation stage is intentionally lightweight. It is meant to catch:

- missing files
- missing key columns
- unreadable regulatory references
- unreadable bigWig headers

It is not meant to fully dry-run MAGMA, dndscv, fishHook, or diffusion.

# Step 2: compute the three score modalities

## Germline scoring with MAGMA

The germline branch uses MAGMA twice, once for genes and once for regulatory
elements.

```{r germline-code, eval=FALSE}
germline_scores <- prepare_germline_scores(
  gwas_sumstats = gwas_path,
  reference_bfile = reference_bfile,
  gene_sample_size = sample_size,
  reg_sample_size = sample_size,
  gene_step1_args = list(annotation_window = c(35, 10)),
  gene_step2_args = list(
    gene_model = "snp-wise=mean",
    pval_use = c("SNP", "P"),
    pval_duplicate = "drop"
  ),
  reg_step1_args = list(annotation_window = c(0, 0)),
  reg_step2_args = list(
    gene_model = "snp-wise=mean",
    pval_use = c("SNP", "P"),
    pval_duplicate = "drop"
  ),
  verbose = TRUE
)
```

The key MAGMA idea is:

- step 1 decides which SNPs belong to each feature
- step 2 decides how SNP-level signal becomes one feature-level score

## Somatic scoring with dndscv and fishHook

The somatic branch is split into a gene model and a regulatory model.

In the current package defaults, the gene branch asks `dndscv` for one-sided
positive and negative selection output when that API surface is available, and
the regulatory branch uses fishHook's directional `p` and `p.neg` columns when
present. That keeps score extraction aligned with the underlying statistical
tests rather than reconstructing direction from a generic two-sided p-value.

```{r somatic-code, eval=FALSE}
somatic_scores <- prepare_somatic_scores(
  maf = somatic_maf_path,
  refdb = refdb_path,
  reg_ref_path = reg_ref_path,
  dndscv_args = list(sm = "192r_3w"),
  fishhook_covariate_data = covariate_dt,
  fishhook_args = list(),
  verbose = TRUE
)
```

When fishHook covariates are used, the practical table shape is:

```{r fishhook-example, eval=FALSE}
covariate_dt <- data.frame(
  reg_elem_id = c("EH38E0080197", "EH38E2084302"),
  accessibility = c(1.2, 0.4),
  replication_timing = c(0.7, -0.1),
  gc_content = c(0.44, 0.51)
)
```

That is, one row per regulatory element, a regulatory-element identifier, and
the covariates you want fishHook to model.

## Epigenomic scoring from bigWig tracks

The epigenomic score in `conseguiR` is best interpreted as a **cross-track
variability score**, not a generic activity score.

```{r epigenomic-code, eval=FALSE}
epigenomic_scores <- prepare_epigenomic_scores(
  reg_ref_path = reg_ref_path,
  bw_files = bw_files,
  min_tracks = 3L,
  drop_mhc = TRUE,
  transform = "log1p",
  summary_fun = mean,
  verbose = TRUE
)
```

# Step 3: build the scored gene-regulatory graph

```{r graph-code, eval=FALSE}
scored_graph <- build_scored_gene_reg_graph(
  germline_scores = germline_scores,
  somatic_scores = somatic_scores,
  epigenomic_scores = epigenomic_scores,
  verbose = TRUE
)
```

At this point, all three modalities have been imposed onto the graph. Missing
modalities are zero-filled by default, which keeps the graph broad enough to
retain biologically meaningful features even when one branch is absent.

# Step 4: diffusion

As in the overview vignette, the diffusion and selected-subgraph stages are
Python-backed internally but are run through conseguiR's managed `basilisk`
environment rather than a user-supplied interpreter.

```{r diffusion-code, eval=FALSE}
diffusion <- run_gene_reg_diffusion(
  scored_graph = scored_graph,
  top_k = 3L,
  confidence_power = 2.0,
  beta_germline = 0.5,
  beta_somatic = 0.5,
  beta_epigenomic = 0.7,
  integration_weight_germline = 1.0,
  integration_weight_somatic = 1.0,
  integration_weight_epigenomic = 1.0,
  reg_signal_clip = 5.0,
  verbose = TRUE
)
```

This stage turns the pre-diffusion graph scores into a gene-centered
post-diffusion ranking that incorporates regulatory context.

The main ranking columns now use a weighted signed Stouffer-style integration
across the three post-diffusion modalities. That means positive germline,
somatic, and epigenomic support reinforce one another, while negative modality
contributions are carried forward and can reduce the final integrated score.

# Step 5: selected subgraph calling

```{r subgraph-code, eval=FALSE}
selected_subgraph <- call_selected_subgraph(
  diffusion = diffusion,
  target_genes = 50L,
  candidate_pool_size = 400L,
  node_prize_weight = 1,
  edge_conf_weight = 1,
  edge_cost_weight = 1,
  verbose = TRUE
)
```

This is the step where the workflow shifts from a full graph to a smaller,
more interpretable network.

# Step 6: figures and interpretation

Below we show representative outputs from the workflow. The code used to
generate each figure is shown directly above it.

## Germline gene plot

```{r code-germline-plot, eval=FALSE}
plot_germline_gene_scores(
  germline_scores = germline_scores,
  stage = "pre",
  label_features = c("MYC", "BCL2", "CXCR5"),
  plot_file_path = "germline_genes_pre_rank.pdf"
)
```

```{r fig-germline, echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("data/processed/figures/_audit_germline_gene_pre.png")
```

This plot gives us a quick read on which genes are leading the germline signal
before diffusion.

## Somatic gene plot

```{r code-somatic-gene-plot, eval=FALSE}
plot_somatic_gene_scores(
  somatic_scores = somatic_scores,
  stage = "pre",
  label_features = c("MYC", "BCL2", "TP53"),
  plot_file_path = "somatic_genes_pre_volcano.pdf"
)
```

```{r fig-somatic-gene, echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("data/processed/figures/_audit_somatic_gene_pre.png")
```

This is where we can see which genes dominate the somatic branch.

## Epigenomic regulatory plot

```{r code-epi-plot, eval=FALSE}
plot_epigenomic_reg_scores(
  epigenomic_scores = epigenomic_scores,
  label_features = c("MYC", "BCL2"),
  plot_file_path = "epigenomic_regs.pdf"
)
```

```{r fig-epi-reg, echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("data/processed/figures/_audit_epi_reg.png")
```

This figure emphasizes the regulatory elements that are most variable across
the selected epigenomic tracks.

## Selected subgraph plot

```{r code-subgraph-plot, eval=FALSE}
plot_selected_subgraph(
  selected_subgraph = selected_subgraph,
  plot_file_path = "selected_subgraph.pdf"
)
```

```{r fig-subgraph, echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("data/processed/figures/_audit_subgraph_tightened.png")
```

This is usually the point where the workflow becomes easiest to discuss with
other people, because we have moved from long ranked tables to a smaller and
more interpretable network view.

## Locus-level plot

```{r code-locus-plot, eval=FALSE}
plot_locus_context(
  chromosome = "8",
  start = 127200000,
  end = 128200000,
  scored_graph = scored_graph,
  diffusion = diffusion,
  gwas_sumstats = gwas_path,
  label_top_lit_snps = 3L,
  label_features = c("MYC"),
  plot_file_path = "sjogren_MYC_locus_context.pdf",
  verbose = TRUE
)
```

```{r fig-locus, echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("data/processed/figures/MYC_locus_context_10labels.png")
```

The locus plot is where the package becomes especially useful for biological
storytelling. It lets us connect:

- regulatory-element scores
- post-diffusion gene scores
- regulatory interactions
- and cited SNPs that overlap the locus

# Closing thoughts

This report is meant to read like an analysis narrative rather than a bare API
listing. The important pattern across the workflow is that each stage adds a
different layer of structure:

- input validation establishes trust in the raw data
- scoring provides modality-specific signal
- graph construction puts all modalities in one shared space
- diffusion makes the regulatory context gene-centered
- subgraph calling distills the large network into something more interpretable
- plotting turns those results into views that can actually support discussion

In practice, that is why `conseguiR` works best as a staged workflow rather
than as a one-line black box.

# Session information

```{r session-info}
sessionInfo()
```
